line_preview: ## SIPS 2017 Sample Size and Effect Size Workshop

*Notes: The fabs package will be submitted soon to CRAN. By the end of August there will be additional useful functions included such as the ability to determine expected power given a certain sample size for the future study (i.e., holding future sample size constant, estimate expected power for that future study) as well as the ability to BYOP (Bring Your Own Posterior) to the calculation such as that based on an informative prior. For instance, by incorporating meta-analytic data with the study effect size estimate you are focused on.*

Download and install the [fabs](https://github.com/jbiesanz/fabs) package from github if you have not done so already. These three lines, assuming you have already installed the `devtools` library in `R`, accomplish that task.
Have fun! Evaluate a simple analysis. Run the code below and see how long it takes to provide an answer. Generally between 20 seconds and a minute depending on the computer.

Run the code again. Is the answer exactly the same? Most likely not as expected power is computed through simulation and stochastic approximation. Generally the answer will not vary meaningfully much across repeated runs. If reproducability is critical, you can always use use the `set.seed()` function. If precision is critical, you can set it up on a loop and average the results from 20 or more runs. That will often get precision down to 2 or 3 decimal places which is well beyond what is required for practical research.
# Will always produce the same answer.

You submitted a manuscript where the primary effect of interest was $r(198) = .32$. The editor strongly requests a pre-registered replication before accepting the manuscript for publication. For the pre-registration what sample size is required to achieve adequate statistical power given the uncertainty associated with your observed effect size?

You want to replicate a study that observed *d*=0.80 with *n*=15 in each group. What sample size will you need in the replication to have expected power of .80? How does this compare to the estimate from either G*Power or `power.t.test`? Note that the `power.t.test()` function in `R` allows you to do a traditional power analysis given a fixed parameter $\delta$ = `delta/sd` (where `delta` and `sd` are parameters that you input into the function).
You want to replicate a study that observed *d*=.40 with *n*=100 in each group. What sample size will you need in the replication to have expected power of .80?
